figures.caption: Table 2: Reconstruction performance comparison of video tokenizers. To evaluate reconstruction quality, we curated a challenging validation set of 160 high-motion videos. For continuous tokenizers, we consider each latent channel to hold 16 bits of information. An HBQ tokenizer with M rounds introduces M bits within each latent channel.
Figure 8: Complexity-Aware Sampling: T2I Qualitative Results.

Figure 13: Uncurated 256×256 samples from GRN-G on ImageNet. To ensure representative results, these images are generated using the same parameters that yielded our reported FID of 1.81 (CFG scale = 1.7, CFG interval = [0.3, 1.0]), rather than using a higher CFG scale typically favored for visualization.
Figure 14: More qualitative results for the text-to-image generation task.

Figure 2: Hierarchical Binary Quantization. Each element from the VAE encoded features undergoes several rounds of hierarchical binary quantization. The quantization error decays exponentially with the number of rounds, theoretically enabling lossless quantization to be achieved rapidly.

Figure 10: Comparison between GRN with other autoregressive models in visual generation. With the global refinement mechanism, GRN iteratively revises and enhances the entire visual representation, effectively mitigating the error propagation issue in conventional autoregressive models.
Table 3: Reference results on ImageNet 256×256. FID [27] and IS [48] of 50K samples are evaluated. Tokenizer “D” refers to a discrete tokenizer, while “C” refers to a continuous tokenizer.
Table 8: Implementation details for our C2I, T2I, T2V models. We elaborate on four key aspects: model architecture, visual tokenizer, training, and sampling.
Table 4: Evaluation of Text-to-Image generation on GenEval [21].
Table 5: Evaluation on the VBench benchmark. † result is with prompt rewriting.
